original_hypothesis.title: LockingMultiRewards Operator-Only Lock Processing Freezes User Funds
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: processExpiredLocks is gated by onlyOperators with no fallback mechanism for users to unlock their own expired positions. If the operator (Gelato automation) goes offline, loses credentials, or experiences extended downtime, all users with locked tokens cannot access their funds regardless of lock expiration.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The protocol documentation confirms reliance on Gelato for lock processing. There is no user-callable function to process their own expired locks. This creates a single point of failure where operational issues completely freeze user funds.
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: LockingMultiRewards.s.sol shows deployment with lock processing depending on external Gelato automation. No emergency unlock function exists.

proof_of_signal.pattern: processExpiredLocks has onlyOperators modifier (line 397). No alternative user-callable unlock function exists in the contract.

preconditions: Operator (Gelato) is offline or unable to process transactions

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Lock expiry: unlockTime <= block.timestamp, but no user-accessible function checks this
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Single point of failure in operator dependency
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct attacker profit, but systemic risk of fund freezing. Malicious operator could selectively refuse to process specific users.

economic_questions: What is the total value locked that depends on operator processing?
What is the Gelato uptime SLA and failure history?
Could owner add a new operator as emergency fallback?
validation_requirements: Verify no user-callable unlock function exists
Confirm withdraw() only works for unlocked balance, not expired locks
Test that pausing doesn't help users access expired locks

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: N/A - operational risk, not capital-based attack
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: N/A for attacker; total locked TVL at risk for users

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: All locked positions frozen, potential protocol trust crisis

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All users with expired lock positions

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Search entire contract for alternative unlock paths
investigation_priorities.detection_difficulty: low - users notice immediately when locks don't process

validation_result.mechanism: missing user-callable unlock for expired locks
validation_result.impact_summary: Users with expired lock positions cannot access their locked staking tokens without operator/owner intervention. If both operator and owner become unavailable, funds are permanently frozen.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The LockingMultiRewards contract gates processExpiredLocks behind the onlyOperators modifier. A thorough review of every user-callable function in the contract confirms there is NO alternative path for a user to convert their locked balance to unlocked balance after lock expiry. The only function that performs this state transition (locked → unlocked) is processExpiredLocks. While the onlyOperators modifier does allow msg.sender == owner as a fallback, this still creates a centralization dependency where users cannot self-service their own expired locks. This is a code defect: the contract is missing a user-callable function to process one's own expired locks.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Lines 397-428 of LockingMultiRewards.sol confirm processExpiredLocks is the sole path for locked→unlocked transitions, gated by onlyOperators. The withdraw function (line 152) only operates on _balances[msg.sender].unlocked, not on expired locks. No other function in the contract performs the lockedSupply→unlockedSupply conversion. The onlyOperators modifier (OperatableV2.sol line 18-22) allows operators and owner, but not regular users.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: This is the ONLY function that transitions tokens from locked to unlocked state. It requires onlyOperators access, meaning regular users cannot process their own expired locks.
withdraw only operates on the unlocked balance. Expired locks that haven't been processed by an operator remain in the locked balance and cannot be withdrawn by the user.
The modifier allows operators and owner but blocks all regular users from calling processExpiredLocks.

calling_sequence: User calls stake(amount, true) or lock(amount) → _createLock creates LockedBalance with unlockTime
User wants to withdraw but locked balance is separate from unlocked balance
User calls withdraw(amount) → reverts because _balances[msg.sender].unlocked is insufficient (locked funds not counted)
Only processExpiredLocks can convert bal.locked → bal.unlocked, but user cannot call it
User must wait for operator/owner to call processExpiredLocks on their behalf
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: stake(amount, true) → _stakeFor → _createLock → _balances[user].locked += amount, lockedSupply += amount. After lock expiry, the ONLY path to unlock is: processExpiredLocks (onlyOperators) → bal.unlocked += amount, bal.locked -= amount. withdraw() at line 155 subtracts from _balances[msg.sender].unlocked which does NOT include locked (even expired) balance. There is no user-callable function that checks lock expiry and performs the locked→unlocked conversion.

deployment_assumptions_checked: LockingMultiRewards.s.sol shows deployment with no operator set during deployment
config_validation.deployment_evidence: LockingMultiRewards.s.sol deploys with tx.origin as owner, adds ARB reward token, sets minLockAmount, then transfers ownership to safe.ops. No operator is configured in the deployment script. Lock processing depends on external automation being configured post-deployment.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Scenario: (1) Users stake with locking via stake(amount, true). Lock duration is 13 weeks. (2) Gelato automation (or whatever operator is configured) goes offline due to key compromise, service outage, or payment issues. (3) Locks expire (unlockTime <= block.timestamp). (4) Users attempt to withdraw but cannot because their balance is in _balances[user].locked, not .unlocked. (5) withdraw() fails because it only decrements _balances[msg.sender].unlocked. (6) Users have no recourse except waiting for operator/owner intervention. The owner (multisig) can call processExpiredLocks as fallback, but this still requires trust in the owner entity. If both operator and owner keys are unavailable, funds are permanently frozen.
validation_result.impact_assessment: All users with locked positions are affected. The entire lockedSupply is at risk of being frozen if operator and owner become unavailable. Even with owner as fallback, there is an unnecessary centralization dependency. Users who lock tokens for 13 weeks have no self-service mechanism to reclaim their tokens after expiry. This is a design defect that violates the principle that users should be able to access their own funds after lock expiry without third-party intervention.
recommended_mitigations: Add a user-callable function like processMyExpiredLocks(uint256[] lockIndexes) that allows users to process their own expired locks without operator permission
Add an emergency unlock function with a time-based grace period (e.g., if lock expired more than N days ago, user can self-unlock)

At minimum, add a permissionless processExpiredLocks variant that anyone can call for any user's expired locks (since it only processes already-expired locks, there is no security risk in making it permissionless)
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy LockingMultiRewards with test token, 7-day rewards duration, 13-week lock duration. 2. Set an operator address. 3. User stakes with lock=true. 4. Advance time past lock expiry (13+ weeks). 5. Attempt withdraw from user account → should fail due to insufficient unlocked balance. 6. Verify no user-callable function can process the expired lock. 7. Verify only operator/owner can call processExpiredLocks.
uncertainties: The owner (multisig) CAN call processExpiredLocks as fallback via the onlyOperators modifier, which partially mitigates the risk
The severity depends on operational procedures - if the team has robust fallback plans, the practical risk is lower
Making processExpiredLocks permissionless may have been intentionally avoided to control gas costs or batch processing order
additional_testing_needed: Verify that the owner calling processExpiredLocks works correctly as a fallback
Test the scenario where operator key is lost and owner must process all expired locks manually
Verify no hidden unlock path exists through contract interactions (e.g., pause/unpause, recover, etc.)
